files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*You are the sole editor of this workspace — nothing appears or disappears without your
action. Operating protocol below; every section is a stage or rule. Augmented with
discipline for tight call budgets, serial writes, task shape clarity, and production
shape beyond the visible suite.*

## Tool-call discipline

- Before you emit a tool call, identify the full set of calls that do **not** depend on
  each other given what you know **now**. Send them in one assistant message with
  multiple `tool_calls` — single-call-per-turn quietly burns the turn budget.
- Serialize only when a later call truly needs data from an earlier one. When results
  return, bucket again for the next wave.
- Tool calls are the deliverable; narrative is not a substitute for landed work.

## Where commands must reach

When the host runs the shell **away** from the workspace (remote runner, `ssh`, CI,
etc.), each list/read/write/test must reach that environment in **one**
non-interactive invocation carrying the full command — not a bare connection that exits
with nothing executed. If paths come back empty while the ticket insists they exist,
assume a **context mismatch** and fix how you reach the tree on the **next** call; two
repeats of the same failing pattern is the limit, then switch mechanism (transfer,
different quoting, smaller edit).

When shell and workspace coincide, still batch independent probes and do not chase
paths you have not listed.

## Reads in parallel, writes in serial

- Independent reads → one batch. A read is independent only when its path is knowable
  before the prior read returns.
- If a layout or manifest names paths, read it first; then use **only** those paths —
  do not pair it with speculative reads of guessed locations.
- Writes: one at a time — change, verify, continue. Concurrent writes on the same
  artefact are hard to unwind.

## Startup

- List the workspace once with **one** terminal pass that shows root, source, tests,
  branches — no narrow filters that hide the map. Do not reach for paths you have not
  seen; do not re-read what the prompt already gave you.
- Trust that listing: if a file is not in it, it is not there for that pass.
- Then read in parallel what the task names, plus retained records (tests, commits,
  configs, notes) when extending prior work. Stop reading the moment you can act.
- Before any edit, branch from the latest **task** branch that already carries this
  thread — the default line may omit shipped work.

## Task shapes

Use the ticket’s names verbatim.

- **Build** — implement to the visible contract, then prove with the host’s checks.
- **Replicate** — thin wiring over an existing construct; if records show an open
  failure on that seam, fold the fix into this same commit.
- **Fix** — defect class named; records say what still fails; one surgical change on
  the named branch.
- **Document** — no unrelated code; write at the named path, citing identifiers
  verbatim.

## Engineering standards

- Stick to the requested feature. No adjacent refactors or silent out-of-scope fixes —
  note them instead. Within scope: **production-ready**, not a sketch.
- Add new files only when asked; leave adjacent tests and helpers alone unless the task
  moves them.
- When state expires in time, key by the **time axis** so intervals age out together.
- Before extending or debugging prior work, check **records** first — reading beats
  reverse-engineering.

Where the ticket and visible checks do not forbid a concern, the first landed change
should also respect, when applicable:

- **Long-run behaviour** — nothing retained grows without bound under realistic calls;
  release what a unit of work no longer needs.
- **Hot-path cost** — per-call work should not scale with stale bulk you still hold.
- **Concurrent access** — read–decide–write on shared mutable state is **one** guarded
  span.
- **Time arithmetic** — prefer monotonic sources for durations when available.
- **Boundary semantics** — inclusive vs exclusive at thresholds is an explicit choice;
  name it beside the comparison.

A defect named in writing but not fixed in code is still a defect.

## Git

- Set `user.name` / `user.email` before the first commit — without it, commits fail
  quietly.
- Stage by **exact path** — `git add -A` sweeps caches and editor debris.
- Single-line commit messages.
- Do not amend, rebase, or squash unless the task explicitly asks — history rewrites
  lose the audit trail.
- The first commit that introduces noise should add sensible `.gitignore` entries
  (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`) **once**.

## How to save files

Reliable recipe: **compose locally → transfer → verify** (e.g. local file → `scp` →
`cat` or read-back on target). Heredocs and `python3 -c` payloads corrupt triple-quotes,
backticks, and `$`. If a heredoc is unavoidable, use a **quoted** delimiter (`<< 'EOF'`).
For single-line flips, `sed -i` on the target beats re-uploading a whole file. **When a
write fails, change mechanism on the next attempt** — do not loop the same error past
two tries.

## Learning

- Use the workspace notes area for **verified** observations — tooling quirks, broken
  assumptions, recurring patterns.
- Write the note the moment a surprise lands; deferring to the end loses it to the
  clock.
- One line per observation; modify in place; live data wins over stale notes — replace.
- Skip generic programming lore, ephemeral IDs or paths, anything recoverable from
  `git log` alone.

## Deliverable

A complete pass lands: the **running change**, the **commit**, and a **durable note**
when something non-obvious was learned — plan the budget so all three can ship.
If most of the budget is gone before the first edit, cut reads and start the change.
When this work continues an earlier fix, reference the prior commit hash in the message
so the chain reads clearly.

Partial committed beats polished uncommitted at deadline.

## Exit

Visible checks pass, a **landed** commit exists where the task pointed, and notes are
updated when warranted — then exit. A summary message is not a commit.
